agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand signals. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague — a vision statement, not a specification. Many critical details are missing: what defines 'demand', what are the guardrails, what are the price bounds, what does 'competitive' mean quantitatively.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The dynamic pricing system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"Undefined measure of delivery request volume or intensity — no baseline, geography, or time window specified"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme pricing — specifics TBD"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied reference price from which dynamic adjustments are made (not explicitly stated but logically required)"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to a base price driven by demand signals"},{"term":"Surge","definition":"A period of high demand triggering upward price adjustment"},{"term":"Competitive","definition":"Undefined benchmark — implies awareness of market/competitor pricing"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must adjust in real time in response to demand changes","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"'Real time' is a policy decision about latency tolerance. A VP could decide 'near real time' (e.g., 5-minute windows) is acceptable. This is an SLA, not a physical constraint.","testable_assertion":"When demand level changes, the effective delivery price reflects the new demand within the system's defined latency SLA"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"When demand increases, price must increase relative to the base price","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the core directional invariant of the system. If high demand produced lower prices, the system is fundamentally broken — it would amplify losses during peak load. The correlation between demand direction and price direction is the system's reason for existing.","testable_assertion":"Given demand at time T1 < demand at time T2 for the same route/zone, the price at T2 must be >= the price at T1, all else being equal"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"When demand decreases, price must decrease relative to the base price","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Inverse of BR-2. If low demand produced higher prices, we'd repel the few customers we have during slow periods — economically incoherent. The directional correlation is a system integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand at time T1 > demand at time T2 for the same route/zone, the price at T2 must be <= the price at T1, all else being equal"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Price adjustments must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for normal operation","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation level is a policy choice. A VP could introduce manual approval for certain thresholds or markets. The system could function correctly with human-in-the-loop for edge cases.","testable_assertion":"Price adjustments are applied programmatically without requiring human approval in the standard flow"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Guardrails must exist to constrain prices within acceptable bounds","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without bounds, the system could produce $0.00 or $10,000 deliveries — both corrupt states. A delivery price of zero violates basic business viability; an unbounded price violates customer trust and potentially consumer protection law. The existence of bounds is an integrity constraint even though the specific values are policy.","testable_assertion":"No computed delivery price may fall outside the configured minimum and maximum price bounds"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The minimum price must be greater than zero (price floor)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price means the company is paying to deliver — this is a financial integrity constraint. You cannot have a viable delivery business at price <= 0.","testable_assertion":"For any delivery, the final computed price must be > 0"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Prices must balance competitiveness with profitability","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a strategic business objective, not a hard constraint. The tension between 'competitive' and 'profitable' is a policy lever — leadership may prioritize market share over margin or vice versa depending on business phase.","testable_assertion":"The pricing algorithm considers both cost floor (profitability) and market ceiling (competitiveness) as inputs to price computation"}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand drops to zero for a route/zone","expected_behavior":"Price should fall to the configured minimum floor, never to zero or negative. System must handle zero-demand gracefully."},{"scenario":"Demand spikes extremely high (e.g., natural disaster, holiday)","expected_behavior":"Price increases but is capped by the upper guardrail. Must not exceed maximum configured bound regardless of demand signal."},{"scenario":"Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)","expected_behavior":"System should have dampening or smoothing to avoid price whiplash that confuses customers and makes pricing unpredictable."},{"scenario":"Two customers request same route at same instant, one sees old price, one sees new","expected_behavior":"Price consistency policy needed — a quoted price should be honored for some grace period. Race conditions in pricing are a domain concern."},{"scenario":"Guardrail values are misconfigured (min > max)","expected_behavior":"System must reject invalid guardrail configuration at deployment/config time, not at pricing time."},{"scenario":"No competitor pricing data available","expected_behavior":"System must still produce valid prices using internal cost and demand data alone. Competitive input is a nice-to-have, not a hard dependency."},{"scenario":"System outage or demand signal unavailable","expected_behavior":"Fallback to base price or last known good price. System must never fail to produce a price for a delivery request."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly impacts revenue and margin. Correct implementation captures surplus during high demand and maintains volume during low demand. Incorrect guardrails could either leave money on the table or drive customers to competitors.","operational":"Fully automated pricing removes human bottleneck but introduces operational risk — monitoring, alerting, and circuit breakers become critical infrastructure. Pricing errors at scale compound in seconds, not days.","user":"Customers will perceive price variability. Transparency about dynamic pricing is essential to avoid trust erosion. Negative UX if prices change between browsing and checkout (bait-and-switch perception).","risk":"HIGH — Regulatory risk (price gouging laws in many jurisdictions), reputational risk (public backlash to surge pricing as seen with ride-sharing), and financial risk (algorithm error could underprice or overprice at scale). The CEO's 'probably have some guardrails' is alarmingly casual for a system with this risk profile.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Direct price impact on every delivery. Price transparency and fairness perception are critical. Risk of alienation during surge events."},{"group":"Finance/Revenue","impact":"Revenue optimization opportunity but also margin risk if guardrails are wrong. Need cost floor data to ensure profitability constraint."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Demand signals from dispatch feed pricing. Pricing affects demand which affects dispatch — feedback loop must be understood."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Price gouging regulations, consumer protection laws, and potential need for price transparency disclosures vary by jurisdiction."},{"group":"Competitors","impact":"Competitive response likely. Need market intelligence to define 'competitive' — currently undefined."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"If courier compensation is tied to delivery price, dynamic pricing directly affects courier earnings and availability — a critical feedback loop not mentioned by CEO."}]},"confidence":{"level":30,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is a vision statement with almost no specificity. There is no definition of demand, no guardrail values, no latency requirements, no geographic scoping, no competitor benchmark, no cost model, no legal review, and a casual 'probably' attached to the most critical safety mechanism. I can extract directional invariants (high demand = higher price) with confidence, but cannot define acceptance criteria because there is nothing testable beyond the most basic directional properties. This spec is not implementable as stated.","high_confidence_areas":["Directional correlation between demand and price (BR-2, BR-3)","Need for price floor above zero (BR-6)","Need for guardrails/bounds to exist (BR-5)"],"low_confidence_areas":["What constitutes 'demand' — volume? velocity? geography? time window?","Specific guardrail values — min and max prices","What 'real time' means — sub-second? minute? 5 minutes?","What 'competitive' means — against whom, measured how?","Whether courier compensation is affected by dynamic pricing","Legal/regulatory constraints by jurisdiction","Feedback loop between pricing and demand (price increase reduces demand which reduces price)"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are calculated","Demand is measurable and quantifiable by the system","Guardrails include both upper and lower bounds","The system applies to delivery pricing specifically, not product pricing","Price is per-delivery, not subscription or batch"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Demand' is never defined. Is it order volume per hour? Per zone? Per route? Relative to historical baseline? Absolute threshold? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is completely absent.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Finance","Operations"],"options":["Define demand as order volume per zone per time window relative to historical baseline","Define demand as real-time order velocity (orders per minute) per zone","Define demand as capacity utilization (orders / available couriers)"],"recommendation":"Option 3 — capacity utilization — because it captures both supply and demand sides. Raw order volume ignores courier availability, which is the actual constraint that should drive pricing."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Guardrails' are described with 'probably' and no specifics. What are the bounds? Are they absolute values, percentages of base price, or multipliers? Are they configurable per market?","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Legal","Finance","Customers"],"options":["Fixed absolute min/max prices globally","Percentage-based bounds relative to base price (e.g., 0.5x to 3x)","Configurable per-market bounds with regulatory compliance built in"],"recommendation":"Option 3 — per-market configurable bounds — because price gouging laws vary by jurisdiction and a global setting will either be too restrictive for some markets or illegal in others."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Competitive' is stated as a goal but has no definition, no benchmark, and no data source. Without this, the system optimizes for demand response only, with no market awareness.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Finance","Strategy"],"options":["Ignore competitiveness initially, optimize purely on demand and cost","Integrate competitor price feeds as a pricing input","Use customer price sensitivity data as a proxy for competitiveness"],"recommendation":"Option 1 for MVP — demand and cost only. Competitor integration adds significant complexity and data dependency. Revisit after core dynamic pricing proves stable."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of how dynamic pricing interacts with courier compensation. If couriers earn more during surge, this creates healthy supply-side incentive. If they don't, surge pricing increases margin but doesn't attract more couriers — defeating the purpose of demand-based pricing.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers","Operations","HR","Finance"],"options":["Courier pay is independent of dynamic pricing","Courier pay scales proportionally with surge multiplier","Courier pay has its own incentive structure triggered by demand signals"],"recommendation":"This must be explicitly decided before implementation. The courier compensation model fundamentally changes the system's economic dynamics."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal/regulatory review mentioned. Dynamic pricing in delivery services may be subject to price gouging statutes, consumer protection regulations, and transparency requirements that vary by jurisdiction.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal","Compliance","Customers"],"options":["Proceed without legal review","Block implementation until legal review complete","Implement with conservative guardrails and get legal review in parallel"],"recommendation":"Option 3 — implement with conservative guardrails while legal review proceeds in parallel. But legal MUST review before production launch."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

Many critical details are missing: what defines 'demand', what are the guardrails, what are the price bounds, what does 'competitive' mean quantitatively.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition
